did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithms to classify skin cancer diseases using dermoscopy images. The study achieved specific accuracy metrics for these AI/ML models on a dataset of 2,239 skin cancer images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a skin cancer classification system using CNN, SVM, and KNN algorithms on a specific dataset of 2,239 dermoscopy images. It includes a dedicated 'Research Method' section detailing data collection, preprocessing, and model training, as well as a 'Results and Discussion' section reporting specific performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.) derived from the authors' experiments. Although it references prior literature, its primary purpose is to report new empirical results rather than summarize existing work.

title: Classification of Skin Cancer Diseases Using KNN, CNN and SVM Methods
journal: Journal of Engineering, Electrical and Informatics
brief_summary: This study aimed to classify nine types of skin cancer diseases using dermoscopy images to assist in early diagnosis. The researchers utilized a dataset of 2,239 images from Kaggle, which included classes such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and nevus. Three machine learning algorithms were evaluated: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN). The models were assessed using metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-Score. The CNN model achieved the highest performance with 98.35% accuracy, 92.59% sensitivity, and an AUC of 0.8891. The study concluded that CNN outperformed SVM and KNN in accurately detecting and classifying skin cancer types.

all_task_types.target: Nine discrete classes of skin cancer diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign, Seborheic Keratosis, Squamos Cell, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly aims to classify skin cancer diseases using CNN, SVM, and KNN algorithms. The task is a multi-class classification problem involving 9 distinct diagnostic categories derived from dermoscopy images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-Score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer 9 Classes ISIC (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopy images downloaded from Kaggle (source: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/nodoubttome/skin-cancer9-classesisic) containing JPG format images of skin cancer diseases.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for training the CNN, SVM, and KNN models. The text states 'totaling 2,239 containing skin cancer images used as training data'.
use.testing: Used for testing. The text specifies 'with the distribution of test data for each class is 15 test images', totaling 135 images (9 classes * 15 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study compared model performance using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-Score without statistical significance tests.

Skin Cancer 9 Classes ISIC (via Kaggle).notes: The CNN model achieved the highest performance among the three algorithms. The study utilized a dataset of 2,239 images for training and 135 images for testing (15 per class). Confusion matrix details were provided for each class within the testing set for all models.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)

conclusions: The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model outperformed Support Vector Machine (SVM) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) in classifying nine types of skin cancer diseases, achieving the highest accuracy of 98.35%, sensitivity of 92.59%, specificity of 99%, precision of 93%, and F1-Score of 93.01%.
The KNN algorithm achieved an accuracy of 90.45%, with lower sensitivity (57.77%) and F1-Score (55.99%) compared to CNN and SVM.
The SVM algorithm achieved an accuracy of 91.17%, with a sensitivity of 61% and F1-Score of 61.26%.

The study demonstrates that AI-based classification methods, particularly CNN, can assist dermatologists in accurately and quickly diagnosing skin cancer types, potentially improving early detection rates and patient survival outcomes.

The research highlights a limitation in existing literature regarding the classification of specific skin cancer types (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesion), justifying the need for this multi-class classification study.
important_remarks: The dataset is sourced from Kaggle (secondary source) with a total of 2,239 images, but the paper does not specify the original provenance, collection protocol, or demographic diversity of the patients, raising concerns about dataset bias and generalizability.
The testing set is extremely small, consisting of only 135 images (15 per class), which limits the statistical power and reliability of the reported performance metrics.

The study employs aggressive preprocessing including grayscale conversion, adaptive thresholding, binary format conversion, and Sobel edge detection. This discards color information, which is critical for dermatological diagnosis, and may introduce artifacts or data leakage if not handled carefully.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal test split from the Kaggle dataset.
Key hyperparameters for the CNN (e.g., architecture depth, layer types, optimizer, learning rate, epochs) and SVM (e.g., kernel type, C parameter) are not reported, making reproducibility difficult.
The K-value for the KNN algorithm is stated to be 'based on available data' but the specific value used is not reported.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance differences between CNN, SVM, and KNN.
The paper mentions developing a website application for clinical assistance, but provides no evidence of clinical validation, user study, or deployment feasibility.
